quizzes.subject: Science
quizzes.title: Physical & Chemical Changes of Matter quiz
quizzes.summary: This quiz follows the slide details about physical changes, changes of state, dissolving, and the exact clues that confirm a chemical change.

questions.prompt: If you tear a piece of paper in half, what kind of change is it and why?
questions.answer: It is a physical change because the substance is still paper and only its form changed.

questions.prompt: Why are changes of state considered physical changes on this slide?
questions.answer: Because the substance stays the same even when its state changes.

questions.prompt: Why is dissolving sugar in water called a physical change here?
questions.answer: Because if the water evaporates, the sugar is still there, so no new substance was formed.

questions.prompt: What is the main sign that a chemical change has happened?
questions.answer: A chemical change forms a new substance.

questions.prompt: What products are given as evidence that burning a candle is a chemical change?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide, water vapour, heat, and light are produced.

questions.prompt: How many clue signs should be present before you confirm a chemical change?
questions.answer: At least two clues should be present.

questions.prompt: Name two clue signs from the slide that suggest a chemical change.
questions.answer: Possible answers include a new colour, bubbles of gas, light or heat being given off, a precipitate forming, or the change being hard to reverse.

questions.prompt: What does the slide say about reversibility in a chemical change?
questions.answer: The change is difficult, if not impossible, to reverse.

questions.prompt: What is a precipitate according to the slide?
questions.answer: It is a solid that forms when two solutions are mixed together.

quizzes.subject: Science
quizzes.title: The Periodic Table quiz
quizzes.summary: This quiz checks the history, periodic law, and arrangement details that appear directly on the periodic table slide.

questions.prompt: About how many elements had been discovered by the 1800s according to the slide?
questions.answer: More than 63 elements had been discovered.

questions.prompt: Which two chemists developed early periodic tables based on atomic mass?
questions.answer: Lothar Meyer and Dmitri Mendeleev.

questions.prompt: Why is Mendeleev known as the father of the periodic table?
questions.answer: Because he published his table first in 1869.

questions.prompt: What does periodic law mean on this slide?
questions.answer: When elements are arranged by atomic number, their chemical and physical properties repeat periodically.

questions.prompt: Where are metals found on the periodic table?
questions.answer: Metals are found on the left side and in the middle of the table.

questions.prompt: Where are non-metals found on the periodic table?
questions.answer: Non-metals, except hydrogen, are found on the right side.

questions.prompt: Where are metalloids found?
questions.answer: Metalloids are found between the metals and non-metals.

questions.prompt: What are the vertical columns called?
questions.answer: They are called groups or families.

questions.prompt: What are the horizontal rows called?
questions.answer: They are called periods.

questions.prompt: What rule does the slide give for chemical symbols?
questions.answer: The first letter is uppercase and the second letter, if there is one, is lowercase.

quizzes.subject: Science
quizzes.title: Coding for Science quiz
quizzes.summary: This quiz covers the micro:bit vocabulary, selection logic, pins, and conductivity testing details that the teacher can pull straight from the coding slide.

questions.prompt: What is an output according to the slide?
questions.answer: An output is something made by a person, system, or machine.

questions.prompt: What does selection mean in coding on this slide?
questions.answer: Selection means a set of actions happens when a certain condition is met.

questions.prompt: Which micro:bit pins can be used as inputs in this lesson?
questions.answer: Pins 0, 1, and 2 can be used as inputs, together with GND.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of connecting another clip to the GND pin?
questions.answer: It helps create a circuit so the pins can work as an input.

questions.prompt: What should you click when testing the code in the simulator after pressing play?
questions.answer: You should click the P0 button.

questions.prompt: After downloading the code, what must you connect to the micro:bit before testing materials?
questions.answer: You connect the battery pack and then the alligator clips to GND and P0.

questions.prompt: What is the coded micro:bit being used as in this experiment?
questions.answer: It is being used as a conductivity tester.

questions.prompt: What property are students testing in the range of materials?
questions.answer: They are testing whether the materials conduct electricity.

questions.prompt: If the condition in the code is met, what can you infer about the material?
questions.answer: You can infer that the material is an electrical conductor.

quizzes.subject: Science
quizzes.title: Atomic Theory quiz
quizzes.summary: This quiz focuses on the order of atomic models, the particle discoveries, and the exact experiment details that often become teacher quiz questions.

questions.prompt: What word did the Ancient Greeks use for indivisible particles?
questions.answer: They used the word atomos.

questions.prompt: What two ideas are part of Dalton's billiard ball model?
questions.answer: Matter is made of tiny indivisible atoms, and atoms of the same element are identical.

questions.prompt: What was one limitation of the billiard ball model named on the slide?
questions.answer: It could not explain why different elements had different masses.

questions.prompt: Which discovery in 1897 led to a new atomic model?
questions.answer: The discovery of the electron.

questions.prompt: What did the plum pudding model say about positive and negative charge?
questions.answer: It said electrons were scattered through a spherical cloud of positive charge, and the atom as a whole was neutral.

questions.prompt: Why did the slide say the plum pudding model was weak?
questions.answer: Because there was no experimental evidence to support it.

questions.prompt: What did Rutherford observe in the gold foil experiment?
questions.answer: Most alpha particles passed through, but some were deflected at very large angles.

questions.prompt: What conclusion did Rutherford make about the atom from that result?
questions.answer: The atom has a tiny, dense, positively charged nucleus and is mostly empty space.

questions.prompt: Which particles were identified later to revise the nuclear model?
questions.answer: The proton and the neutron.

questions.prompt: What does the revised nuclear model say about electrons in a neutral atom?
questions.answer: Electrons move rapidly through empty space around the nucleus, and a neutral atom has equal numbers of electrons and protons.

quizzes.subject: Science
quizzes.title: The Gas Tests quiz
quizzes.summary: This quiz checks the exact gas properties, splint tests, and lab reminders from the gas tests slide.

questions.prompt: Why does the slide say gases can still be identified even though many are colourless and odourless?
questions.answer: Because their chemical properties differ even if their physical properties seem similar.

questions.prompt: What test identifies oxygen?
questions.answer: A glowing splint test identifies oxygen.

questions.prompt: What result tells you oxygen is present?
questions.answer: The glowing splint relights.

questions.prompt: What property of hydrogen is emphasized on the slide?
questions.answer: Hydrogen is highly combustible.

questions.prompt: What happens when hydrogen is ignited according to the slide?
questions.answer: Hydrogen combines with oxygen in the air to form water molecules.

questions.prompt: What sound is often heard during the hydrogen test?
questions.answer: An explosive pop sound.

questions.prompt: What test identifies hydrogen?
questions.answer: A flaming splint test identifies hydrogen.

questions.prompt: What does carbon dioxide do to a flame?
questions.answer: It extinguishes the flame.

questions.prompt: Why is carbon dioxide found in many fire extinguishers according to the slide?
questions.answer: Because it does not support combustion and it puts out a flame.

questions.prompt: What two reminders are given before starting the gas test lab?
questions.answer: Follow the instructions carefully and record observations accurately.

quizzes.subject: Science
quizzes.title: Atom Building Activity quiz
quizzes.summary: This quiz is built from the Build an Atom activity details about protons, neutrons, electrons, ions, isotopes, and what changes an element.

questions.prompt: Which particle decides what element an atom is?
questions.answer: The number of protons decides the element.

questions.prompt: What does a neutral atom always have in equal numbers?
questions.answer: A neutral atom has the same number of protons and electrons.

questions.prompt: What changes when you add a neutron but do not change the protons?
questions.answer: The mass number changes, but the element stays the same.

questions.prompt: What changes when you add or remove electrons?
questions.answer: The charge changes.

questions.prompt: What is an isotope?
questions.answer: An isotope has the same number of protons but a different number of neutrons.

questions.prompt: What is an ion?
questions.answer: An ion has the same number of protons but a different number of electrons.

questions.prompt: If the element name changes in the simulation, which particle number changed?
questions.answer: The number of protons changed.

questions.prompt: If only the charge changes, which particle number changed?
questions.answer: The number of electrons changed.

questions.prompt: If only the mass changes, which particle number changed?
questions.answer: The number of neutrons changed.

quizzes.subject: Science
quizzes.title: Bohr-Rutherford Diagrams quiz
quizzes.summary: This quiz covers the drawing rules and shell details from the Bohr-Rutherford slide, including what goes in the nucleus and how to place electrons.

questions.prompt: What two particles are found in the nucleus?
questions.answer: Protons and neutrons are found in the nucleus.

questions.prompt: How many electrons fit in the first shell for the Grade 9 model?
questions.answer: The first shell holds 2 electrons.

questions.prompt: How many electrons fit in the second shell for the atoms used here?
questions.answer: The second shell holds up to 8 electrons.

questions.prompt: What does the atomic number tell you?
questions.answer: The atomic number tells you the number of protons.

questions.prompt: What must be true about protons and electrons in a neutral atom?
questions.answer: A neutral atom has the same number of protons and electrons.

questions.prompt: What does mass number equal?
questions.answer: Mass number equals protons plus neutrons.

questions.prompt: When drawing a diagram, what should you place in the centre first?
questions.answer: You should place the protons and neutrons in the nucleus first.

questions.prompt: What should you check after drawing the shells for a neutral atom?
questions.answer: Check that the number of electrons matches the number of protons.

questions.prompt: If you change only the neutrons in a Bohr-Rutherford diagram, what kind of atom change are you showing?
questions.answer: You are showing an isotope.
